title: Closed Fracture (Simple Fracture)
symptoms: Severe pain that worsens with movement
Inability to bear weight or use the limb
immediate_steps: Call emergency services if the injury involves the head, neck, back, pelvis, or upper leg
Keep the limb completely still
Support the injured area using rolled-up blankets, clothing, or a splint only if trained and moving the person is necessary
Apply an ice pack wrapped in a cloth for 20 minutes to reduce swelling
Elevate the limb if it can be done without causing pain
avoid: Do not try to realign the bone or push it back into place
Do not move the person if a spinal injury is suspected
Do not test the limb's mobility
Do not give food or drink in case surgery is needed

title: Open Fracture (Compound Fracture)
symptoms: Deep wound over a suspected fracture
immediate_steps: Call emergency services immediately
Control bleeding by applying gentle pressure around (NOT directly over) the protruding bone using a sterile dressing
Cover the entire wound and protruding bone with a sterile dressing or clean cloth
Immobilize the area exactly as you found it
Treat the person for shock (keep them warm and calm)
avoid: Do not push the bone back into the skin
Do not probe the wound or attempt to clean it
Do not apply direct pressure on the bone itself
Do not let the person eat or drink

title: Spinal Injury (Suspected)
symptoms: Numbness, tingling, or loss of control in limbs
Head or neck in an unnatural position
immediate_steps: Call emergency services immediately
Instruct the person not to move
Stabilize the head and neck by placing your hands on both sides of the head to keep it in the position you found it
Keep the person calm and warm
If you must move them (e.g., vomiting, choking), log-roll them as a single unit with the help of others
avoid: Do not move the person unless absolutely necessary for their immediate survival
Do not attempt to straighten their neck or back
Do not remove their helmet if they are wearing one

title: Fracture - Identification and Immediate Action
symptoms: Inability to move the affected part
immediate_steps: Keep the injured person still and avoid moving the affected area
Immobilize the injured part using a splint or any rigid support to prevent further damage
Seek immediate medical assistance
avoid: Do not attempt to realign or push the bone back into place

title: Fracture - Splinting and Immobilization
immediate_steps: Use rigid materials such as sticks, cardboard, rolled newspapers, or folded cloth to support the injured area
The splint should extend beyond the joints above and below the fracture site
Secure it gently with cloth or bandages without cutting off blood circulation
Always check for signs of circulation such as warmth, color, and sensation in the limb after splinting
avoid: Do not tighten excessively, as it may restrict blood flow

title: Fracture - Open Fractures and Bleeding Control
immediate_steps: Cover the area with a clean, sterile cloth or dressing to reduce contamination
Apply gentle pressure around the wound to control bleeding
Keep the injured person still and calm
Seek emergency medical care immediately
avoid: Do not push the bone back into the wound
Avoid pressing directly on exposed bone

title: Fracture - Transport and Medical Care
immediate_steps: Transport the injured person to a medical facility as soon as possible
Support the injured limb during transport to prevent unnecessary motion
If possible, keep the person lying down and monitor for signs of shock
Immediate professional medical evaluation is necessary for proper bone alignment and treatment
avoid: Movement should be minimized to avoid worsening the injury
Do not give food or water if surgery may be required
